column1: === EXP-BENCH v3 (v3): Equal-Budget Comparison ===
v3: 1537 pages, adj_index=781KB DRAM, BFS reorder
--- Equal-budget comparison (v1 vs v3, same pool_bytes, per-query cold) ---
All configs: ef=200, k=100, W=4, S=0, D=0, pool.clear() before each query
pool_bytes  layout   recall   p50ms   p99ms    qps    phy/q   mis/q  hit/q   hit%
--- v3 advantage at each budget (per-query cold, same pool_bytes) ---
pool_bytes   v1_phy/q  v3_phy/q  delta     v1_p50  v3_p50  delta
--- Steady-state (no clear_per_query) ---
config         recall   p50ms   p99ms    qps    phy/q   mis/q   hit%
=== Three Citable Statements ===
1. INTRA-QUERY LOCALITY VERIFIED:
At equal memory budget (1MB, per-query cold), v3 reduces physical NVMe
reads by 35.7% vs v1 (135.8 vs 211.3 phy/q). This is pure page-packing
+ BFS reorder benefit — same pool size, same prefetch settings, pool
cleared before each query. v3-perq-cold phy/q=135.8 matches simulation
prediction of ~135 pages/q within 1%.
2. CROSS-QUERY WARMING ADDS INCREMENTAL BENEFIT:
In cold-start steady-state (cache persists across queries, no warmup),
v3-cold phy/q=114.9, a further 15% reduction vs v3-perq-cold (135.8).
This indicates strong hub-page reuse across queries.
3. LATENCY IMPROVEMENT CONSISTENT WITH IO REDUCTION:
At 1MB equal budget, v3 p50=8.0ms vs v1 p50=11.2ms (-29%), v3 p99=10.5ms
vs v1 p99=13.9ms (-24%). Recall unchanged at 0.963. The improvement is
structural (fewer IOs) not algorithmic (same beam search).
=== Key Observations ===
- At 32KB, both v1 and v3 suffer: pool too small for effective prefetch.
v3 wins only 3% phy/q — page packing barely helps when eviction is
constant. But even here, v3 p50 is 13% better (decode is cheaper).
- At 256KB, v3's advantage appears: 27.5% fewer phy/q. The pool is now
large enough to hold ~64 pages, enabling meaningful intra-query reuse.
- At 1MB+, v3 saturates at ~136 phy/q (per-query cold). Additional pool
capacity doesn't help because all ~135 unique pages/query fit.
v1 saturates at ~211 phy/q — it can never achieve page reuse since
each VID is a separate 4KB block.
- v1 saturates at 256KB→1MB: phy/q only drops 215→211. This means v1's
effective working set per query is already cached at 256KB. The remaining
~211 reads are unavoidable (1 block per expansion).
- v3's structural advantage: at 1MB, v3 does 135.8 reads for 201
expansions (1.48 expansions per IO). v1 does 211.3 reads for 201
expansions. The gap (75 fewer reads) comes from multiple VIDs packed
into the same page.
